fact: January 14 is exactly 2 weeks after New Year's Day - that's when most people's resolutions start falling apart!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is smack dab in the middle of winter's deepest freeze, when even polar bears think it's chilly.
The sun rises super late and sets embarrassingly early on January 14 in northern places - some kids go to school in the dark and come home in the dark too!
January 14 falls right in the heart of Capricorn season, when the sea-goat constellation rules the zodiac with its stubborn, mountain-climbing energy.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere spots, January 14 is peak summer - perfect beach weather while the rest of us are bundled up like marshmallows!
Tree sap moves slower than molasses on January 14 in cold regions, basically putting trees into a deep winter coma.
Many hibernating animals are in their deepest sleep around this date - bears are snoring so loud they could wake the neighbors!
The moon phase on January 14 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, the winter sky becomes absolutely magical and bright enough to read by.
In northern countries, the air is so cold and dry around January 14 that your breath creates instant fog clouds that look like dragon smoke.
January 14 sits exactly 159 days away from the summer solstice in the north - that's like counting down to the longest, most awesome day of the year!
Penguins in Antarctica are having their busiest parenting season around January 14, waddling around with chicks and looking like tiny tuxedo-wearing families.
The Earth is actually closer to the sun around January 14 than in summer, but the Northern Hemisphere is tilted away, which is why winter still feels like a freezer!
